content: The Earth goes around the Sun in a big circle, and it also spins like a top. But Earth is a little tilted, like it’s leaning to one side. Because of this tilt, different parts of Earth get different amounts of sunlight during the year. When the top half of Earth, called the Northern Hemisphere, is tilted toward the Sun, it gets more sunlight and feels warmer. This is called summer for people living there. At the same time, the bottom half, the Southern Hemisphere, is tilted away from the Sun, getting less sunlight and feeling colder, which is winter for them. Six months later, the Earth has moved halfway around the Sun, and the tilt points the other way. Then, the Northern Hemisphere has winter, and the Southern Hemisphere has summer. That’s why we have seasons like spring, summer, fall, and winter! It’s all because of Earth’s tilt and its trip around the Sun.
